coredata.dc:title: Modeling and experimental validation of hydrodynamics in an ultrasonic batch reactor
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Ajmal, M.
Rusli, S.
Fieg, G.
coredata.dc:description: Simulation of hydrodynamics in ultrasonic batch reactor containing immobilized enzymes as catalyst is done. A transducer with variable power and constant frequency (24kHz) is taken as source of ultrasound (US). Simulation comprises two steps. In first step, acoustic pressure field is simulated and in second step effect of this field on particle trajectories is simulated. Simulation results are compared with experimentally determined particle trajectories using PIV Lab (particle image velocimetry). Effect of varying ultrasonic power, positioning and number of ultrasonic sources on particle trajectories is studied. It is observed that catalyst particles tend to orientate according to pattern of acoustic pressure field. An increase in ultrasonic power increases particle velocity and also brings more particles into motion. Simulation results are found to be in agreement with experimentally determined data.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Hydrodynamic
Ultrasound
Sonotrode
Enzymes
Esterification

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Boundary conditions for particle tracing module
Geometry, domain, boundary conditions and meshing

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Effect of non-cavitating and cavitating US on immobilized enzymes.
Transducer with horn and glass reactor.
3-D geometry for reactor with one transducer.
Boundaries in simulation model in reactor.
Mesh for acoustic pressure calculations (3-D).
Setup for particle image velocimetry measurements [IDT (UK) Ltd].
Acoustic pressure field with Sonotrode in center.
Particle trajectories in reactor, with Sonotrode at center.
Influence of ultrasonic power to particle velocity in reactor.
Particle velocity distribution in reactor for different power inputs.
Acoustic pressure field in reactor, with 7mm Sonotrode off-center.
Particle trajectory in reactor, with Sonotrode offcenter.
Reactor with multiple Sonotrodes.
Acoustic pressure field in reactor with multiple Sonotrode.
Particle distribution for reactor with 1, 3 and 5 Sonotrode (each transducer fed with 80W).
Velocity of 75% particles in reactor with multiple Sonotrodes.
Particle velocity distribution for reactor with 1, 3 and 5 Sonotrodes (total power 80W).
Particle velocity distribution for reactor with 3 Sonotrodes (effect of spacing b/w Sonotrodes).
Comparison between simulation result and experiment data for reactor.
Calorimetric method for measurement of ultrasonic power.
Properties of example reaction system.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Effectiveness factor is introduced to account for losses occurring in ultrasonic transducer.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Independent variables in simulation models.

head.ce:title: Modeling and experimental validation of hydrodynamics in an ultrasonic batch reactor

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: University of Technology Hamburg, Institute of Process and Plant Engineering, Am Schwarzenberg-Campus 4 (C), 21073 Hamburg, Germany

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Process and Plant Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Simulation of hydrodynamics in ultrasonic batch reactor containing immobilized enzymes as catalyst is done. A transducer with variable power and constant frequency (24kHz) is taken as source of ultrasound (US). Simulation comprises two steps. In first step, acoustic pressure field is simulated and in second step effect of this field on particle trajectories is simulated. Simulation results are compared with experimentally determined particle trajectories using PIV Lab (particle image velocimetry). Effect of varying ultrasonic power, positioning and number of ultrasonic sources on particle trajectories is studied. It is observed that catalyst particles tend to orientate according to pattern of acoustic pressure field. An increase in ultrasonic power increases particle velocity and also brings more particles into motion. Simulation results are found to be in agreement with experimentally determined data.

ce:section.ce:para: In heterogeneously catalyzed reactions high-intensity ultrasound produces agitation, which can be utilized to disperse particles [1]. At liquid/solid interfaces, acoustic waves cause turbulence known as “acoustic streaming” [2,3]. This reduces the diffusion boundary layer, increases the convective mass transfer, and considerably accelerates diffusion [4]. These effects influence external resistance and should affect mass transfer in the same way as mechanical stirring [5].

Use of enzymes for catalyzing chemical reactions is becoming popular. The key advantages associated with enzymes include their high activity and specificity (thus minimizing or eliminating side product formation) along with capability to operate at milder operating conditions (thus decreasing the energy consumption of process) [6]. For industrial applications enzymes are bound on some porous carrier material termed as immobilized enzymes (this facilitates separation of enzyme from product). Study of enzyme catalyzed reactions is a major topic of research at Institute of Process and Plant Engineering TU Hamburg [7–12]. Research involves both experimental as well as modeling aspects. Objective of the research is to develop strategies for efficient utilization of enzymes for industrial scale applications. This requires investigation of the methods to intensify the activity of enzymes. One such method is application of ultrasound. A review of literature shows that application of ultrasound to enzyme catalyzed reactions can bring an improvement of up to 200% [13–17]. In order to investigate this phenomenon in detailed and systematic manner a research project is started at institute. This project will involve experimental study leading to the development of a mathematical model for an enzyme catalyzed batch ultrasonic reactor. Current work presents the first step towards the development of such a model.

One of the requirements for effective utilization of US is that the applied US should be uniformly dispersed in the medium (acoustic pressure field). Meeting this requirement helps in improving the efficiency of chemical reaction by achieving the highest possible process intensification. This requires a thorough understanding that how different influencing parameters effect this acoustic pressure field (which ultimately governs the reactor performance). Some of the influencing parameters include ultrasonic power, frequency, position of ultrasonic source, type of reacting medium, reactor geometry, material of construction of reactor, temperature etc. Experimentally determining the influence of these parameters on acoustic pressure field for a given reaction requires a lot of time and investment. Moreover doing so, experimentally for every new reaction and reactor geometry is certainly impractical. A practical approach is to develop a mathematical model that can predict the performance of an ultrasonic reactor as a function of afore mentioned parameters. This model can be validated for a small scale lab reactor. Once validated, it can be adapted for predicting the performance of new reactors and geometries [18].

Simulation of ultrasonic reactors using COMSOL has been presented in a number of publications e.g. [19–24]. In all of these publications, simulations are limited to the distribution of sound pressure (acoustic pressure field) in reactor. To the best of our knowledge, it is first ever study to simulate the effect of ultrasound on immobilized enzyme particles. In the present study a simplified method for simulation of an enzyme catalyzed (immobilized) ultrasonic batch reactor is presented. Effect of ultrasonic power, transducer position and use of multiple Sonotrodes is studied. To validate the simulation results “particle image velocimetry (PIV)” has been used. Once validated, model can be used to predict the distribution of particle trajectories as a function of afore mentioned parameters. From the simulation it is possible to predict the trajectories of catalyst particles in reaction medium resulting from sonication. The hydrodynamic information thus obtained can be used to estimate rate of mass transfer. In this way performance for newly proposed ultrasonic reactors can be analyzed quickly and conveniently.

Patidar and Kalva [25,35] have modeled the phenomenon of cavitation in sonicated enzyme catalyzed reaction systems. They have shown that cavitation bubbles can influence reaction through physical (turbulence, shock wave) and chemical (radical formation) effects. They stated that cavitation has an adverse effect on enzyme action (due to shock waves) and therefore should be avoided. For a given power input of ultrasound, detrimental effects of cavitating ultrasound can be avoided by using a higher hydrostatic pressure [25,36]. Our experiments in lab have also shown that cavitating US damages Novozymes-435 particles (used as example for present study). Novozyme-435 was sonicated (80W) in lab at 40°C in equimolar mixture of oleic acid and n-hexanol for two hours. As can be seen in Fig. 1 enzyme particles got damaged due to cavitation and therefore it was not possible to reuse them. However under same conditions but in absence of cavitation the enzyme particles remained undamaged. Due to these facts cavitation phenomenon is undesirable and therefore not considered in simulation.

Example batch reactor used in this study consists of a transducer (ultrasonic source) and a glass reactor (Fig. 2 ). Transducer used is UP400S from Hielscher US Technology and is capable of operating at a constant frequency of 24kHz (400W). Ultrasonic generator and transducer are integrated into one assembly. For transfer of US into reaction medium ultrasonic horns are attached to the transducer. The transducer assembly can be mounted on a stand to sonicate the reaction medium. To hold the reaction contents a glass reactor from NORMAG Labor- und Prozesstechnik GmbH is employed. The glass reactor has a heating/cooling jacket around it to maintain the required temperature. Ultrasonic horn can be inserted into reaction medium at desired positions (vertically and horizontally).

Immobilized enzyme used in this study was Novozym-435. In Novozym-435 lipase enzyme is immobilized on Lewatit VP OC 1600, a divinylbenzene-cross-linked poly (methyl methacrylate) resin produced by Lanxess Germany. The average particle size is around 0.6mm with pore size around 4.5nm [26].

In current study application of ultrasound to a suspension of immobilized enzyme particles in reaction medium is to be modeled. For this two-step modeling approach is adopted as
1.
Acoustic modeling: Simulates the dispersion of ultrasound in reaction medium.
2.
Modeling of particle trajectories: Simulates how particles are affected by the acoustophoretic force (resulting from acoustic pressure).
In the following paragraphs follows a detailed explanation about each model.

The governing equation for propagation of sound inside a medium is the Helmholtz wave equation given by [18,20]
(1)
∇
1
ρ
∇ p
-
ω
2
ρ
c
2
· p = 0
where ρ =density of medium [kg/m3], p =acoustic pressure [Pa], c =speed of sound in medium [m/s],
ω = 2 π f
(angular frequency)[rad/s],
f
=frequency [Hz].

The above mentioned equation is in time-harmonic formulation. Sound wave travels in harmonic manner, so the time dependence can be taken out of the equation. Using this equation, sound field is described and solved by the pressure p. The pressure represents the acoustic variations (or excess pressure) to the ambient stationary pressure. Acoustic pressure gives the acoustophoretic force (F aco) for particle movement in Section 3.2.

•
Sound hard boundary (reactor walls): This type of boundary assumes that incident sound wave is perfectly reflected back and is in phase with the incident one (R =1). Wall of reactor employed is made of glass. Yasui et.al. have simulated the acoustic pressure field in ultrasonic reactor using different wall boundaries (rigid and thin/thick glass) [28]. They have shown that reactor with thicker glass wall acts as a rigid boundary. For the present study a reactor with thick walls has been taken in order to ensure sufficient strength while doing experiments under higher pressure. Therefore use of sound hard boundary (rigid boundary [29]) is justified in this case. (marked blue in Fig. 4
).
•
Impedance boundary (reactor top): This type of boundary assumes that incident wave is partially reflected and partially transmitted i.e. −1< R <1 (marked green in Fig. 4)
where
(2)
R =
Reflected
pressure
Incident
pressure
=
Z
2
-
Z
1
Z
2
+
Z
1
Reflection coefficient
[ - ]

•
Pressure boundary is to define the ultrasound source (transducer) in the liquid medium. It is a Dirichlet Boundary Condition, where the pressure on the boundaries is set to be the pressure caused by ultrasonic transducer. Pressure is calculated from ultrasound intensity with following equation [2]
(3)
p =
( 2 ∗ I ∗ ρ ∗ c )
where Z =
ρ ∗ c
[impedance of medium kgs/m2], I =intensity of sound source [w/m2].
Intensity mentioned above was measured experimentally. Details of the experimental procedure are given in Appendix A.

Modeling of particle trajectories’ resulting from ultrasound is done using an equation based on Newton’s law of motion (the net force on an object is equal to the derivative of linear momentum). For particles inside the sonicated batch reactor, Newton’s law is formulated as
(4)
d
dt
(
m
p
v ) =
F
g
+
F
aco
Main forces involved in particle movement are gravity force (F g), and acoustophoretic force (F aco from the acoustic pressure).

The gravity force (F g) is defined according to Eq. (5),
(5)
F
g
=
m
p
g
(
ρ
p
- ρ )
ρ
p
where
F
g
,
F
aco
=gravitational/acoustophoretic force [N], g =gravity constant [m/s2],
ρ
p
=density of particles [kg/m3],
ρ
=density of medium [kg/m3],
m
p
=mass of catalyst particle.

The acoustophoretic force (F aco) is defined in Eq. (6) as,
F
aco
= - ∇
U
rad

rad
=
V

f
1

2 ρ

2
(

in
2
) -

)

1
= 1 -
ρ

;

2

2 (

+ ρ

i ω ρ
∇ p
(6)

1

p
2
;

2 (
ρ

2

+ ρ
v
in

The acoustophoretic force is a special subclass of the CFD forces, which are the acoustic radiation forces on small particles. This implies that the expressions are valid for particles of diameter (0.6mm) smaller than the acoustic wavelength (57.5mm at 24kHz) and larger than acoustic boundary layer thickness defined as [30–32]
(7)
δ
bl
=
ν
ω
= 0.01
mm ( 10
μ m )

From the above comparison of particle size with wavelength and boundary layer thickness it is obvious that the necessary condition for applicability of above mentioned correlations for acoustophoretic force is fulfilled.

Where
U
rad
=radiation potential [J], p in =incident external pressure field [pa], v in =incident particle velocity amplitude [m/s], c p =compressional speed of sound in particle [3000m/s], V p =volume of particles [m3], δ bl =boundary layer thickness [m], ν =kinematic viscosity [m2/s].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Boundary conditions for particle tracing module

ce:list-item.ce:para: Wall boundary: Wall boundary condition defines, what happens to the particles upon coming into contact with wall of reactor. For present study bounce condition is used which means all particles are bounced back in the direction they come from. Marked blue, red and green in Fig. 4.

ce:section.ce:para: In particle tracing, initial position of the particles is taken to be randomly distributed; with initial particle velocity v 0 =0 for every direction. It is also assumed that there are no interactions between particles.

Above mentioned equations are solved in COMSOL multiphysics as it has a unique capability to allow coupling of two different physics (acoustic and particle tracing in this case). Acoustic calculation is solved in frequency domain (time-independent), while particle tracing is time-dependent. Simulations are calculated in two steps. First acoustic pressure field is calculated (independent of particle movement). In second step particle tracing model is solved where acoustic pressure field is used to calculate the movement of particles.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Geometry, domain, boundary conditions and meshing
ce:section.ce:para: The first step of simulation in COMSOL after setting up the appropriate model equations is to build the geometry of the system. 3-D geometry of the reactor used in this study is shown in Fig. 3. Position of the Sonotrode is also drawn. For each variation of transducer position new geometry is required to be drawn (which also requires re-meshing).

After drawing the geometry next step is to specify domain properties. For present study, domain consists of equimolar mixture of oleic acid and 1-hexanol. It is a homogeneous mixture at a temperature of 40°C. Material properties required for simulation are specified in Table 1 .
Fig. 4 represents the cut off plane from 3D simulation of reactor. Different boundary conditions are specified with different colors.

In order to solve an FEM problem meshing is required which divides entire domain into small cells/elements. Type of meshing used has a great influence on the results of simulation. Therefore, an optimum mesh is vital in order to get reliable simulation results. For example acoustics module has a certain DOF requirement, depending on its frequency. To get a reliable result, the wavelength has to be resolved by mesh of the geometry. At higher frequencies, wavelength gets shorter and accordingly a finer mesh is required in order to accommodate this small wavelength. On the other hand convergence problems arise with too fine mesh as it deforms due to the movement of particles. So it is important to have a sufficiently fine mesh in order to resolve wavelength but also coarse enough to allow movement of particles with certain diameter.

The reactor for this study operates at lower frequency, therefore coarser mesh can be used and hence it is relatively easy to run a 3-D simulation of this reactor. The mesh used for the reactor is shown in Fig. 5 .

To validate results of simulation catalyst particle velocities were measured using particle image velocimetry (PIV). In PIV particle movement is captured by a high-speed camera (motion pro Y4 shown in Fig. 6 ). The camera is capable of capturing up to 5000 frames per second (fps). The images are then analyzed using a particle image velocimetry program in MATLAB, called PIVlab. The program detects the changes in position of particles between two consecutive images. From the distance traveled by the particle, the program determines the speed of particles in the region of interest (ROI).

In this section results of simulation are presented and discussed. In Table 2 parameters of simulation are given. As shown in table, transducer is operated at a constant frequency of 24kHz. The variables investigated are ultrasonic power and position of transducer. The possibility of using multiple Sonotrodes is also investigated.

The results from the simulations are velocity of each individual particle inside the reactor, at different time step. Because only a few particles have high velocity, the data has to be treated in a statistical manner; and therefore it is presented in histograms. A histogram shows how many particles have a certain velocity at a certain physical time step. The more particles with lower velocity, the more positively skewed the histograms are [33].

Series of simulations are needed to investigate the influence of variables in Table 2. To have comparable data sets, simulation results have to be taken at steady state condition. Determination of steady state can be done by plotting the results in the form of histograms (particle velocity) at different time steps. When the histograms are stable (no variations over time), it can be concluded that the system has reached steady state. For comparison, the data sets are taken at the same time steps.

For sonicated reactor, there are two aspects of simulation results:
1.
First is the analysis of acoustic pressure field created by ultrasound which affects the movement of particles in reactor. This leads to different particle trajectories depending upon the field pattern.
2.
Second is the analysis regarding magnitude of particle velocity resulting from acoustophoretic force. The results are then compared to experimentally determined data.

Fig. 7
shows the acoustic pressure field (a) and corresponding particle trajectory (b) simulated for employed reactor at 24kHz and 80W, with 7mm Sonotrode (positioned in the middle of reactor).

From comparison of acoustic pressure field and particle trajectories, it can be observed that applied ultrasound is capable of keeping catalyst particles in suspension and as expected the particles orientate according to acoustic pressure field (between regions of low and high pressures). Distribution of these low and high pressure regions is strongly influenced by frequency. For the applied frequency the wave length is 57mm, which means that distance between higher and low pressure regions is large. Comparing it to dimensions of the reactor it is obvious that there will be few regions of higher and lower pressure. If we want to have more regions of higher and low pressure we will have to decrease wavelength by increasing frequency (detailed investigation of this effect is topic of a further study). Fig. 8 is a comparison of median particle velocity in reactor, for different power inputs i.e. 20, 40, and 80W. As expected an increase in power input to the reactor causes an increase in particle velocity. However, this increase in velocity from one power level to next is not significant. In Fig. 9 a histogram for particle velocity distribution at three different power inputs is shown. Here is also obvious that when we go from one power level to the next we get more particles with higher velocity. However, this increase in number of particles with higher velocity is not significant. Therefore, we cannot expect a uniform particle velocity distribution by inserting single US source into medium.

Kalva et al. [35] have modeled the cavitation phenomenon for biodiesel production and have reported that bulk liquid velocity generated by cavitation bubbles in oil phase is around 0.00277m/s. Although the operating conditions and reaction systems used are different but the reported value can be used for a qualitative comparison. From the comparison of velocities it is obvious that the contribution of cavitation towards over all convection in the reactor is smaller.

As mentioned earlier, in the reactor Sonotrode can be positioned anywhere inside medium. A simulation was run by putting Sonotrode to off center position (3cm from the center). This change in position is likely to effect the spreading of US and thus the trajectory of particles. Fig. 10a and b
show the corresponding US and particle dispersion. The simulations are done with 7mm Sonotrode at 80W power.

When the Sonotrode is moved from the center to the off-center position, mixing pattern is changed. In Fig. 7b, particles are distributed uniformly around the Sonotrode, while in Fig. 10b they are aggregated towards the side opposite to transducer. Some particles are even stuck at the bottom of the reactor on the opposite side of the Sonotrode. This phenomenon is undesirable, since it diminishes the contact between catalyst and the reactant and therefore is likely to result a lower conversion.

As seen in previous simulation results, use of a single Sonotrode does not produce uniform particle distribution. This leads to the idea of using more than one Sonotrode inserted at different positions inside reactor (Fig. 11 ). Based on this idea, simulations are run for two different arrangements.
1.
Arrangement 1 with 3 Sonotrodes [7mm each].
2.
Arrangement 2 with 5 Sonotrodes [7mm each].

Two different studies are done for each arrangement. In one study each Sonotrode is fed with 80W power. In the second study 80W power is equally divided among Sonotrodes (This means in arrangement-1 each Sonotrode gets 26.6W, while in arrangement-2 each Sonotrode gets 16W). Acoustic pressure field for a simulation with 5 Sonotrodes is shown in Fig. 12 . Velocity distributions in the form of histograms are shown in Figs. 13–15
.

Fig. 13 proves the improvement of particle velocity distribution caused by adding more transducers. The histogram with three Sonotrodes has shifted towards right, meaning that there are more particles with higher velocity. A similar trend can be observed using five Sonotrodes. This means with multiple Sonotrodes, the acoustic pressure field and the corresponding particle trajectories are more evenly distributed in comparison to the single one. If we compare the velocity based on individual particles, it can be seen that velocity of 75% of particles in each case is higher (Fig. 14).

However, the particle velocity between 3 Sonotrodes and 5 Sonotrodes shows only slight improvement. Considering the investment and the power requirement, reactor with 3 Sonotrodes is more efficient than reactor with 5 Sonotrodes. Fig. 15 shows particle velocity distribution with multiple Sonotrodes but with lower power input. It can be seen that here again the reduction in power results in a decrease of particle velocity. From this it can be concluded higher power input is desirable in order to ensure proper enzyme particle suspension.

While using multiple Sonotrodes it is important to consider the interference between waves produced by different sources. Abbaszadeh et.al [34] have studied the influence of multiple Sonotrodes on ultrasound dispersion and noted that a maxima of ultrasound pressure occurs at a spacing of 3λ/2. For case under study calculated wavelength is 57.5mm at frequency of 24kHz. Looking the dimensions of reactor it is not possible to give a spacing of 8.625cm between transducers. For a configuration with three transducers simulations with varying distance between transducers were done. The results of simulation are shown in Fig. 16 . From the results it is obvious that when the spacing between transducers is increased from 0.522λ to 0.3λ, curve is shifted towards right side, meaning more particles with higher velocity. This proves that spacing between ultrasonic sources is also an important factor while developing an ultrasonic reactor with multiple sources.

To validate the model, results from simulation are compared to the particle velocity measured from PIVLab. Fig. 17 shows the comparison between particle velocity obtained from simulation and experiment.

A comparison of simulation and experimental data shows that both data have similar tendency i.e. by increasing the power input, the particle velocity increases. The values from simulation are higher in comparison to the experimental one (for example particle velocity from COMSOL at 160W is around 29% higher in comparison to experimentally measured velocity). The discrepancy b/w simulated and experimental data can be attributed to the following factors
•
Difficulties in detecting the particles: As seen in Fig. 6, camera only detects particles closest to reactor walls. It is difficult to detect particles in the middle of the reactor, where the particles with highest velocities are located as transducer is positioned in the middle of rector. This is the reason why simulation results have higher maximum velocity than the data from experiment.
•
Two dimensional analyses in PIVLab: High speed camera takes two dimensional images of the reactor. Two dimensional images of a round object are distorted on the edges, leading to possible miscalculation of the velocity (Fig. 6).

Simulation of hydrodynamics for a lab scale ultrasonic batch reactor using COMSOL is successfully demonstrated. Following conclusions can be made based on the results
•
The results of simulation are validated against experimental data measured by particle image velocimetry. Looking the accuracy of experimental arrangement used it can be said that experimental data is reasonably comparable.
•
Middle position of ultrasonic source gives better distribution of ultrasonic energy in comparison to the off-center position. This results in better particle suspension and higher contact between catalyst and reactants. So a reactor with middle position of US source will give better performance.
•
It is also seen that increasing the ultrasonic power results in higher velocity of particles. However, the velocity distribution is not normalized and there are fewer particles with higher velocity. From this it can be concluded that the effect of ultrasound is localized and not widespread. Adding more ultrasonic sources at different positions in batch reactor causes more particles with higher velocity. However if we look from industrial scale reactors, it is not practical to keep on adding US sources while increasing the size of reactor. This requires the development of new concepts for effective use of ultrasound in industrial sale reactors which requires systematic and thorough experimental investigations.
Next step is to make a comparison between particle trajectories resulting from stirring and ultrasound. This will be the topic of our next paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Authors would like to acknowledge the financial support from German Federal Ministry of Economics and Technology through ZIM program (Zentrales Innovationsprogramm Mittelstand, Project number: KF 2267708MF2).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
According to user manual transducer can provide power between 0 and 400W. However this power specified by manufacturer (P Transd) is not actually transferred to the liquid medium in the form of ultrasound as there are losses in transducer. Therefore actual ultrasonic energy going into liquid is less and can be calculated according to Calorimetric method Fig. A1
[37].

A thermometer was positioned inside reactor. Sonication was started and rise in temperature (resulting from ultrasound) was noted against time. After measuring heating rate, transducer was turned off and as a result the temperature of the medium starting falling (due to heat losses from reactor wall). This rate of cooling was also recorded. This rise and fall in temperature was then plotted against time. From the slope of the heating/cooling lines rate of energy input was then calculated according to following correlation
(A1)
P
US
=
mc
p
Δ T
Δ t
Heating
-
Δ T
Δ t
Cooling
[ W ]

From measured power it was possible to calculate an “effectiveness factor” for the used device,
(A2)
η =
P
US
P
Transd

Therefore
(A3)
P
US
= η
P
Transd
[ W ]

Intensity can be calculated by dividing through area of ultrasound emitting surface as
(A4)
I
US
=
P
US
A
transd
=
η
P
Transd
A
transd
[ W /
m
2
]
Once we know the intensity we can calculate ultrasonic pressure as

Intensity and ultrasonic pressure are correlated according to the correlation
(A5)
I
US
=
( p )
2
2 ρ c
[ W /
m
2
]

So the ultrasonic pressure can be written as
(A6)
p =
2 ρ
cI
US
=
2 ρ c
η
P
Transd
A
transd
[ kPa ]

Calculation of effectiveness factor (0.40 in present case) in simulation facilitates user. User need to specify rated power only and effective source pressure will be calculated automatically once simulation is started.

Commercial scale biocatalysis: myths and realities
Bioorg. Med. Chem.
